title: Music Theory
description: Notes, scales, intervals, tempo markings, dynamics, and musical terms

cards.back: Also called G clef; used for higher-pitched instruments and the right hand of piano
Also called F clef; used for lower-pitched instruments and the left hand of piano
The five horizontal lines on which musical notes are written

cards.front: Lines of the treble clef (bottom to top)
cards.back: E, G, B, D, F ('Every Good Boy Does Fine')

cards.front: Spaces of the treble clef (bottom to top)
cards.back: F, A, C, E (spells 'FACE')
4 beats
2 beats
1 beat
1/2 beat
Raises a note by a half step
Lowers a note by a half step
Cancels a sharp or flat
The smallest interval in Western music (e.g., E to F, or C to C♯)
An interval of two half steps (e.g., C to D)
The interval between one note and the next note with the same name (8 notes apart)
A sequence of notes in ascending or descending order
Whole, whole, half, whole, whole, whole, half (W-W-H-W-W-W-H)
Sounds sad or dark, compared to the brighter major scale
A scale using all 12 half steps within an octave
The sharps or flats at the beginning of a staff that indicate the key of the music
Two numbers at the beginning of music: top = beats per measure, bottom = note value that gets one beat
Common time — 4 beats per measure, quarter note gets one beat
Waltz time — 3 beats per measure, quarter note gets one beat
The speed at which music is played
Fast tempo
Slow, leisurely tempo
Walking pace; moderately slow tempo
Very fast tempo
Moderate tempo
Very slow, broad tempo
Gradually getting louder
Crescendo
Gradually getting softer
Decrescendo (diminuendo)
Soft dynamic marking
Loud dynamic marking
Very loud
Very soft
Moderately loud
Moderately soft
Three or more notes played simultaneously
A three-note chord built in thirds (e.g., C-E-G)
The combination of simultaneous musical notes to produce chords
A sequence of single notes that form a recognizable tune
The pattern of sounds and silences in time
A symbol indicating a period of silence in music
A symbol indicating a note or rest should be held longer than its normal duration
Go back to the beginning of the piece
Notes played short and detached
Notes played smoothly and connected
Staccato = short and detached; Legato = smooth and connected
